ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for stir-fried flat rice noodle dish with chicken and Thai sauce. Mapped to closest parent 'noodle dish' class (FOODON_00002187) as the most defensible available anchor.
ontology.foodex2_description: Stir-fried noodle dish — rice noodles with meat
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP (pasta and noodle dishes) used as closest parent category. No specific code exists for Thai-style stir-fried flat rice noodles with chicken in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific stir-fried noodle dish preparation. SNOMED CT does not provide granular food composition codes at this level.

main_ingredients: flat rice noodles (hor fun / kway teow)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of stir-fried chicken hor fun weighs approximately 500 g, as listed on the GrabFood menu for this item.

nutrient_source.title: Health Promotion Board Singapore — Nutrient Composition of Food (Foodo Database); supplemented by USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for component cross-validation
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board, Singapore; United States Department of Agriculture
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single HPB Foodo entry exists for Thai-style stir-fried chicken hor fun. Values are adapted from HPB Foodo entries for stir-fried flat rice noodles with chicken (char kway teow / hor fun variants) and cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cooked rice noodles, stir-fried chicken, and common Southeast Asian sauces. The input record's per-100g values (energy 150 kcal, protein 7 g, fat 6 g, carbohydrate 17 g, sodium 360 mg) are consistent with HPB Foodo data for similar stir-fried flat noodle dishes and are retained with medium confidence.

gi_source.title: International table of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2002 — Foster-Powell K, Holt SHA, Brand-Miller JC; supplemented by University of Sydney GI Database entry for rice noodles
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Thai-style stir-fried chicken hor fun. GI of 61 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for rice noodles (fresh/cooked, GI ~61, medium category). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (61 × 85.0) / 100 = 51.85 ≈ 52.0 g, where carbohydrate per 500 g serving = 17.0 × 5 = 85.0 g. Stir-frying with protein, fat, and vegetables moderately lowers the effective glycemic response compared to plain boiled rice noodles.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content at 360 mg per 100 g, equivalent to 1800 mg sodium per standard 500 g plate, from combined fish sauce, oyster sauce, and soy sauce
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit this dish to a small portion or request sauce on the side, as a single 500 g plate delivers approximately 1800 mg sodium — 78% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2300 mg — with insufficient potassium or dietary fibre to offset the sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI refined flat rice noodles (estimated GI 61, medium-high) with 17 g carbohydrate per 100 g and only 0.8 g dietary fibre, yielding a high glycemic load of approximately 52 per standard 500 g plate
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should reduce the noodle portion to no more than 150–200 g, increase the proportion of chicken and non-starchy vegetables, request no added sugar in preparation, and pair with a low-GI food to blunt the postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (1.6 g per 100 g), trace trans fat (0.04 g per 100 g), and high sodium (360 mg per 100 g) with cholesterol at 36 mg per 100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish in moderation; the saturated fat and sodium levels are concerning at full-plate portions, and requesting reduced sauce and oil during preparation can meaningfully lower both the sodium and fat load.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High sodium from multiple sauces may worsen RA-related tissue oedema; garlic and chilli paste provide anti-inflammatory polyphenols but chilli may trigger sensitivity in some individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should request reduced sauces to lower sodium intake and monitor personal tolerance to chilli paste, which contains capsaicin that may provoke sensitivity in some RA patients.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Stir-fried Chicken Hor Fun with Thai Sauce is a Singapore hawker-style dish featuring broad, silky flat rice noodles (hor fun or kway teow) wok-fried at high heat with sliced chicken, bean sprouts, Chinese chives, garlic, and a Thai-influenced sauce blend of fish sauce, oyster sauce, soy sauce, and chilli paste. The dish is sold under the '711D' label on GrabFood Singapore, indicating a specific hawker stall variant. The noodles are soft and slightly chewy with a smoky wok-hei aroma; the sauce is savoury, mildly spicy, and umami-rich. Per 100 g, the dish provides 150 kcal, 7 g protein, 6 g fat (1.6 g saturated), 17 g carbohydrate, 0.8 g dietary fibre, and 360 mg sodium. The high sodium content (1800 mg per standard 500 g plate) makes it a concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease. The refined rice noodle base has an estimated glycemic index of 61 (medium), yielding a high glycemic load of approximately 52 per plate, making portion control essential for individuals with type 2 diabetes. The dish is dairy-free but contains gluten (soy sauce, oyster sauce), shellfish derivatives (oyster sauce), and fish (fish sauce). It is a popular lunch and dinner option in Singapore's multicultural food landscape, reflecting the cross-cultural Thai-Singaporean culinary influence common in hawker centres and food delivery platforms.

confidence_reason: No single approved source entry exists for this specific Thai-style stir-fried chicken hor fun product. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Foodo Singapore entries for similar stir-fried flat rice noodle dishes with chicken and cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component data. The input record's values are internally consistent and plausible for this dish type. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for rice noodles. Confidence is medium due to the adapted rather than exact source match.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific Thai-style stir-fried chicken hor fun preparation are not available in the HPB Foodo Singapore database or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as of 2024 for this exact dish variant. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.04 g per 100 g from the input record (GrabFood source); this trace value is retained as it is plausible for a stir-fried dish using commercial cooking oil. The dish is sold as a GrabFood Singapore menu item under the '711D' label; the portion size of 500 g is sourced from the GrabFood menu listing. Halal status is listed as false as this cannot be confirmed from available data; consumers should verify with the specific stall. The rheumatoid arthritis health context note in the input record incorrectly stated 'No trans fat and no deep-frying' and 'Deep-fried preparation generates AGEs' in the same entry — these contradictory statements have been corrected in the output. GI of 61 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database data for cooked rice noodles. Ontology match types set to 'closest_parent' for both FoodOn and FoodEx2 as no exact match exists for this specific dish preparation.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Drafted full RESULT JSON. Identified that the input record's health_suitability used 'diabetes' key instead of 'type2_diabetes' — corrected to use 'type2_diabetes' machine key with 'Type 2 Diabetes' human_condition_name. Added Cardiovascular Disease health context (missing from input). Added Thai local_name (mandatory for Thai-influenced dish). Added Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi local names (mandatory for Singaporean dish). Resolved contradictory rheumatoid_arthritis cautions in input. Added GI estimate from University of Sydney GI Database. Documented all -1 fields in notes. Verified aliases consistency between flat array and aliases_by_language.
Iteration 2: Corrected ontology.foodon_match_type and ontology.foodex2_match_type from 'analogous_estimate' to 'broader' to align with allowed values for G5 hard gate.

Iteration 3: Corrected ontology.foodon_match_type and ontology.foodex2_match_type from 'broader' to 'closest_parent', which is the correct allowed value per the validator's enumerated list ['closest_parent', 'exact', 'none']. Updated notes to reflect this correction. All other fields and criteria remain valid and pass.
